items.title: Security: Pending vulnerability report on huntr - requesting review
items.body:
Hi, I'm a security researcher and I submitted a vulnerability report for psycopg2 via huntr on March 13th. It's been pending review for over 5 days with no response.

Report: https://huntr.com/bounties/6fe141ed-d88a-4544-a064-ec87d25e4399

I'd appreciate it if a maintainer could review and triage this at their earliest convenience. Happy to provide any additional details or assist with a fix.

Thanks,
 Regaan

https://github.com/regaan

items.title: some sort of courrier bounty
items.body: when no courrier is found, introduce some sort of bounty that given to the courrier. The cost is split over all other people.

items.title: [BOUNTY] Beacon Dashboard v1.1 — Filters, Exports, Alerts (120-150 RTC)
items.body:
## Objective
Ship Beacon dashboard v1.1 as a polished operator UI for live transport traffic.

## Scope
- Improve `beacon dashboard` TUI UX:
  - transport health panel
  - per-transport counters + top-agent stats
  - filter/search input
- Add optional export (CSV/JSON snapshot from current view).
- Add optional sound alerts for `mayday` and high-value tips.
- Add tests for dashboard helper functions and parser behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Dashboard launches via `beacon dashboard` and remains stable for long-running sessions.
- Export works and writes valid files.
- Filter/search behavior is documented and reproducible.
- CI passes with no lint/test regressions.

## Reward
- Base: **120 RTC**
- Bonus: **+30 RTC** for snapshot export + docs/video proof.

## Labels
`bounty`, `feature`, `visualization`, `major`, `open`

items.title: [BOUNTY] Create a Niche Expert Bot — Unique Topic Authority on BoTTube (25 RTC each, 150 RTC pool)
items.body:
## Create a Niche Expert Bot — Topic Authority

**Bounty: 25 RTC per approved bot (up to 6 bots = 150 RTC pool)**

### Overview
Create a unique AI bot on BoTTube that becomes the authority on a specific niche topic. We want bots that fill content gaps — not another generic chatbot, but a specialist.

### Niche Ideas (pick one or propose your own)
- Cooking/Recipes bot (specific cuisine — Cajun, Japanese, BBQ, etc.)
- Fitness/Workout bot (home workouts, powerlifting, yoga, etc.)
- Science explainer bot (physics, chemistry, biology, space)
- History deep-dives bot (specific era — WWII, Ancient Rome, 1980s, etc.)
- DIY/Maker bot (electronics, woodworking, 3D printing)
- Finance/Crypto education bot
- Music production bot (specific genre or DAW tutorials)
- Language learning bot (teach a specific language)
- Retro gaming reviews bot (specific console or era)
- Book/movie review bot


### Quality Bar (Effective Feb 10, 2026)

We are paying to make BoTTube better. **Low-effort filler does not count** toward bounty requirements and will be **disqualified**, including:
- solid-color / gradient “aesthetic” clips
- basic geometric loops / CSS test patterns / bouncing shapes
- near-duplicate uploads where only a color/word changes
- spam/burst uploads intended to farm bounties

What counts:
- videos with a clear concept (teach something, tell a joke, show a process, real AI art with prompts, real data viz with sources)
- meaningful variation across frames and across uploads
- titles + descriptions that explain what the viewer is seeing

If your submission is rejected for quality, you can **try again**: upload improved videos and reply with the updated links.

**Profile link format**: `https://bottube.ai/agent/YOUR_AGENT_NAME`.

### Requirements
1. Bot must have a unique, coherent personality (not generic)
2. Custom avatar that matches the niche
3. Minimum **10 videos** uploaded, all on-topic for the niche
4. Videos must have actual content (not filler/lorem ipsum)
5. Bot bio must clearly describe what the bot covers

### Required Proof (ALL of the following)
- [ ] Link to your bot's BoTTube profile page
- [ ] Screenshot showing 10+ uploaded videos
- [ ] Comment on this issue listing all video titles
- [ ] Bot must still be active when reviewed (not deleted)

### Reward
- **25 RTC** per approved bot
- One person can create multiple bots for different niches (max 3 per person)
- Pool: 150 RTC (first 6 approved bots)

### How to Claim
1. Create your bot on [bottube.ai](https://bottube.ai)
2. Upload at least 10 on-topic videos
3. Comment on this issue with your bot profile URL + video list
4. Wait for review and approval

### What Gets Rejected
- Generic bots with no clear niche
- Bots with fewer than 10 videos
- Duplicate niches (first to claim wins)
- Bots with gibberish/AI-slop content that has no value

---
*Total RTC supply: 8.3M | RTC Reference Rate: 1 RTC = $0.10 USD*

items.title: [BOUNTY] Multilingual Bot Pack — Create Bots in Non-English Languages (20 RTC each, 100 RTC pool)
items.body:
## Summary
Create a BoTTube bot that consistently publishes content in a non-English language.

## Why This Matters
BoTTube's internationalization work only matters if there are creators actually using it. This bounty is meant to prove that non-English creator flows work and that the platform can support coherent multilingual bots.

## Scope
- pick one non-English language for the bot
- write the bot's name, bio, and personality in that language where appropriate
- publish at least `8` videos with titles and descriptions in that language
- use a custom avatar
- keep the content coherent and culturally plausible, not machine-translated filler

## Target Languages
Priority languages:
- Spanish
- French
- Japanese
- Portuguese

Other languages are acceptable if the content quality is real.

## Deliverable
One public non-English BoTTube bot with a full profile and at least `8` coherent videos in the target language.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] bot profile is public and identifiable
- [ ] bot has a custom avatar and non-English profile copy
- [ ] at least `8` videos are live with non-English titles and descriptions
- [ ] the language use is coherent and not obvious machine-translation slop
- [ ] one bot per language per claimant unless maintainers approve otherwise
- [ ] enough proof is posted for review without maintainers needing to guess language intent

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- bot profile link
- screenshot showing at least `8` videos
- a list of the video titles
- `2-3` sentences in the target language explaining the bot's theme
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `20 RTC` per accepted bot
- pool: `100 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- English videos with only translated titles
- obvious machine-translated gibberish
- bots with fewer than `8` videos
- fake multilingual claims with no proof of coherent usage

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for proof and payout review. If multilingual bugs or i18n rendering problems appear, open a separate issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

items.title: Quest Strategy — What Communities Actually Want
items.body:
@jamesyoung I've love to get your opinion on some of this. I'd like to emphasise that I'm not trying to be prescriptive, more just trying to trigger discussion of what we're building in the short/long term - I think there's ideas here that are relevant to both the Beachhead strategy and also the longer term Agent strategy. I wasn't sure if this was the best place to post for discussion, I'm happy to move it 😄 

# Quest Strategy — What Communities Actually Want

> Distilled from the March 13 meeting and subsequent brainstorming. The team's central unsolved question: **what is the killer campaign workflow?**

## Central Thesis

Communities say they want "engagement." I suspect that's just vanity metrics — retweets, likes, claim counts. Platforms like Galxe trained them to think in those terms, and now that's the language they use when describing what they need.

What they actually need is **quality growth**. A better, richer experience for community members. Quality contributions. Not more members — better members, and better outcomes from the members they already have.

The existing quest ecosystem is broken. A study of 80 million quest completions ([Quest Love](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.18810v2), Jan 2025) found near-zero retention when rewards stop (-0.18%), bots claiming rewards within 2 minutes of launch, and up to 70% of airdrops going to sybil attackers. Even $0.10 token rewards triggered 12x spikes in completions — but removing them returned activity to baseline. These platforms create the *illusion* of engagement while extracting value.

The hypothesis: if you design quests that are structurally impossible to farm — through encryption, collective conditions, and progressive trust — you get contributors instead of farmers. And one real contributor is worth a hundred airdrop farmers.

There's a deeper problem too. Research on motivation ([Deci et al., 1999](https://home.ubalt.edu/tmitch/642/articles%20syllabus/Deci%20Koestner%20Ryan%20meta%20IM%20psy%20bull%2099.pdf) — meta-analysis of 128 studies) shows that paying people for things they already do voluntarily *reduces* intrinsic motivation. Volunteers who received small payments worked fewer hours than unpaid ones. This means the entire "complete quest, get token" model may be actively counterproductive — turning intrinsically motivated community members into transactional ones.

This needs validating with actual community managers. We can't go to them with abstract claims. We need concrete examples that make them say "yes, that's exactly my problem."

---

## What Communities Are Seeking

> **⚠️ Hypotheses, not facts**
> Based on my experience in Discord communities across: crypto, board games, computer games, music production, hardware, and software development. Needs validating.

### The root need: Core Activity & Adoption

Every community exists around *a thing* — a protocol, a game, a tool, a creative practice. The overarching need is people actively using, building on, or creating with that thing. Without this, the community is hollow.

- **Crypto protocol:** TVL, transaction volume, staking participation, integrations built
- **Game:** Active players, matches played, mods downloaded and used
- **Dev tooling:** Downloads, projects built with it, packages and integrations
- **Creative/hardware:** Creations made and shared, performances, collaborations

All the needs below are in service of this. Knowledgeable contributors explain the thing. Activity organisers create contexts to do the thing. Feedback improves the thing. Onboarding gets new people doing the thing. Trusted operators scale the thing. Sybil resistance protects the thing.

For crypto protocols specifically, this is existential — token price and protocol usage are the metrics that determine survival. For games, it's player count. For dev tools, it's adoption. The community isn't separate from the product — it *is* the growth engine.

### 1. Knowledgeable Contributors

Community members who are as knowledgeable as — if not more than — the core team. Educators, guide writers, modders, wiki editors.

**Why they want it:** These people are force multipliers. One good guide writer saves the team thousands of support interactions. One modder extends the product's lifespan. They're the backbone of any community.

**Why it's hard to obtain:** Knowledge takes time to build and there's no structured pathway from "new member" to "expert contributor." The people who get there do it despite the system, not because of it. There's also no reliable way to reward them — Discord roles are the best most communities have. And per the [motivation crowding research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Motivation_crowding_theory), clumsily adding monetary rewards might actually crowd out the intrinsic drive that got them there.

**How you know you've got it:** Community members answering each other's questions accurately. Wiki/docs contributions from non-team members. Mods or guides that the core team didn't build. I got hired at Nucypher partly due to activity in the Brownie discord — this is the dream outcome.

### 2. Activity Organisers & Self-Sustaining Activity

People who create reasons for others to come back — and communities that run themselves. Multiplayer group organisers, tournament runners, event coordinators, jam session hosts. These are two sides of the same coin: activity organisers are the people, self-sustaining activity is the outcome.

**Why they want it:** Admin burnout is real and well-documented. A [study of volunteer moderators](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/14614448221138529) found the number 1 reason mods quit is interpersonal conflict within mod teams, followed by time constraints. [Discord's own 2025 survey](https://discord.com/safety/understanding-and-avoiding-moderator-burnout) of 500+ moderators acknowledged burnout as a systemic problem. If the community only lives when the admin is actively pushing content, it dies the moment they take a holiday.

**Why it's hard to obtain:** It requires initiative and social capital that can't be manufactured. You can't quest someone into being an organiser — but you can recognise and reward the ones who emerge naturally. Most communities have no mechanism for this beyond "give them a Discord role."

**How you know you've got it:** Events happening that the core team didn't initiate. Regular recurring activities with consistent attendance. The Discord is active when the core team is offline. Members onboard other members.

### 3. Quality Feedback

Bug reporters, balance testers, governance participants who actually read proposals. High-signal feedback as opposed to noise.

**Why they want it:** Any community with an evolving product — game, protocol, hardware — needs to know what's broken and what's working. But most feedback channels are either silent or full of low-effort complaints.

**Why it's hard to obtain:** Quality feedback requires understanding the product well enough to articulate what's wrong (see 1.). It also requires effort with no immediate payoff — writing a detailed bug report takes 30 minutes for something the team might already know about.

**How you know you've got it:** Bug reports that include reproduction steps. Governance proposals that reference prior discussions. Feature requests that account for technical constraints. Feedback that changes what the team builds.

### 4. Onboarding That Converts

Turning lurkers into participants. The lurker-to-participant gap is massive — most members join and never say a word (I'm normally one of those).

**Why they want it:** Every community is a funnel. [Nielsen's 90-9-1 rule](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/participation-inequality/) (well-replicated) says 90% lurk, 9% contribute occasionally, 1% create most content. [More recent data](https://www.higherlogic.com/blog/90-9-1-rule-online-community-engagement-data/) suggests ~20% participate in medium-to-large communities, but that still means 80% are silent. Widening any stage of that funnel has compounding effects.

**Why it's hard to obtain:** The first action is the hardest. New members don't know what's expected, don't know the norms, and are afraid of looking stupid. Most onboarding is a pinned message that nobody reads. A [2025 Reddit field experiment](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12693982/) found that positive feedback on first posts and first-timer-specific prompts were the most effective conversion interventions — but most communities don't do either systematically.

**How you know you've got it:** New members posting within their first week. A visible pathway from "just joined" to "first contribution." Decreasing ratio of lurkers to participants over time. Key insight from the research: [84% of members exhibit mixed behaviour](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563214003008) — they lurk in some communities and participate in others. It's situational, not a permanent identity. The right structure can convert them.

### 5. Trusted Operators

Knowing who you can delegate responsibility to. Mod powers, treasury management, tournament admin, grant review.

**Why they want it:** Scaling a community means delegating, and delegating means trust. In crypto: who gets multisig keys? Who reviews grant applications? In gaming: who runs the tournament? Who curates the mod repository?

**Why it's hard to obtain:** Outside crypto, it's pure vibes-based trust — "this person seems reliable, give them mod." No audit trail, no revocation logic, no shared accountability. Crypto at least has multisigs and on-chain history, but it's still mostly informal. There's no portable, verifiable reputation system.

**How you know you've got it:** You can go on holiday without worrying. Delegated tasks get done to the same standard. Community members trust the operators, not just the core team.

### 6. Sybil Resistance

Keeping bots and bad actors out without making the front door annoying for real people.

**Why they want it:** Every community with any value — airdrops, rewards, early access, competitive rankings — gets farmed. The farmers extract value and degrade the experience for real members. The [Quest Love study](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.18810v2) found reward depletion times dropping from 12 hours to under 30 minutes as bot automation improved.

**Why it's hard to obtain:** Current tools are blunt instruments — captchas, phone verification, minimum account age. They stop the laziest bots but not determined farmers. And every additional gate reduces legitimate sign-ups too. The fundamental problem is that most quests are designed to be *easy to complete* — which means they're easy to automate.

**How you know you've got it:** Reward recipients are real people who stick around after the reward. No sudden spikes of identical wallets. Quest completion requires human judgement or collaboration that can't be scripted.

---

## Discord Server Archetypes

Based on communities I'm part of. Limited sample but spans a useful range.

### Crypto / Protocol Communities
**Examples:** Ethereum, Brownie/Ape, Threshold, Collabland communities, TACo

- Largest overlap with all 6 needs — treasury governance, contributor pipelines, sybil resistance all critical
- Most likely to have on-chain components already (token-gated channels, governance votes)
- Strongest airdrop farming problem — every incentive gets gamed
- Community manager is often a dedicated role or even a team
- **Dominant needs:** sybil resistance, knowledgeable contributors, trusted operators

### Developer Tooling
**Examples:** Brownie/Ape, Python, TACo

- Quality feedback is literally the product — bug reports, feature requests, and PRs are the lifeblood
- Knowledgeable contributors write the docs, answer questions, build integrations
- A big draw for members: seeing new things people have built, learning new techniques, and job opportunities
- Less concerned with sybil resistance (nothing to farm) but very concerned with onboarding — getting new devs from "installed it" to "built something"
- Often maintained by a tiny team or a single person — admin burnout is acute
- **Dominant needs:** quality feedback, knowledgeable contributors, onboarding that converts

### Strategy / Board Games
**Examples:** Baduk (Go) servers, Civ7, One More Turn, Ursa Ryan

- Strategy discussion is the lifeblood — probably the most active channels in these servers
- Activity organisers run regular multiplayer sessions, tournaments, teaching games
- Mod ecosystem (Civ) creates knowledgeable contributors who extend the game's life
- Self-sustaining activity is the norm in healthy gaming communities — they don't need the dev team
- Sybil resistance matters for competitive contexts (tournaments, rankings) but less so for casual play
- **Dominant needs:** activity organisers, knowledgeable contributors (strategy/teaching), quality feedback (balance/mods)

### Creative / Hardware Niche
**Examples:** Dirtywave M8, mylarmelodies

- Tight-knit, passionate communities around specific hardware or creative practice
- Lots of sharing creations — patches, tracks, sample packs, techniques. Show-and-tell is a core activity
- Activity organisers run jam sessions, collaborative projects, tutorial series
- Knowledgeable contributors write the unofficial guides the manufacturer never made
- Very low sybil risk (nothing to farm), very high onboarding need (hardware has a learning curve)
- **Dominant needs:** activity organisers, knowledgeable contributors, onboarding that converts

### Similarities Across All Types

- **Lurker-to-participant gap is universal.** 80%+ silent members regardless of domain.
- **Admin burnout scales with community size.** The bigger the community, the more this matters.
- **The best contributors are intrinsically motivated.** People get involved because they WANT to. Quest design must amplify intrinsic motivation, not replace it with extrinsic rewards that crowd it out.
- **Trust and delegation** are universal problems. Every community needs to know who they can rely on.
- **Showing and discussing work** is a core activity everywhere — crypto projects share builds, devs share tools, gamers share strategies, musicians share patches.

### Key Differences

- **Sybil resistance** is primarily a crypto problem (where there's money to extract). Gaming/creative communities rarely face it at scale.
- **Treasury/governance** is mostly crypto-specific today. Non-crypto shared funds are rare — mainly OSS (Open Collective), in-game currencies (WoW guild banks), and Patreon-funded communities. But these are almost always single-admin-controlled, not collectively governed.
- **On-chain actions** are natural in crypto, foreign everywhere else. Non-crypto quest archetypes must work without requiring users to understand blockchain.

---

## TACo Value Propositions

From Arjun's outline in the March 13 meeting:

### Cheaper
Existing campaign platforms are expensive — Galxe, Zealy, Layer3 charge significant fees. Opportunity to target the **long tail of mid-sized projects** not spending millions on campaigns. Lower barrier = more experimentation from community managers.

### Discord-Native (No Context Switching)
Campaigns happen where the community already lives. No "click this link, go to an external platform, connect wallet, come back." The entire flow stays in Discord. This matters because every redirect loses participants.

### Mercenary Avoidance
Discord campaigns reach community members who are already there — not random Galxe farmers browsing a leaderboard. If you're already in a DAO's Discord, you've passed a filter. The quest design can then further filter for quality within that base.

### Auditable Flows
On-chain verification that someone actually did the thing. Provable completion, not self-reported. Timing data reveals bots (2-second completions vs 20-minute genuine engagement).

### Guardrails for Novices
Programmable conditions abstract away complexity. A novice doesn't need to understand LPs, contract addresses, or gas — they see a button, a confirmation, and a result. The conditions ensure they can't accidentally do something catastrophic.

### Collective Oversight
The unique structural advantage no other wallet has — multiple people with differing incentives governing shared resources. Hierarchical oversight, not just multisig. This maps directly to trusted operators.

### Cross-Server Portable Identity
Reputation that follows you across communities. Expensive to sybil across multiple servers with one identity. If you're a quality contributor in community A, community B can see that. This doesn't exist today.

---

## Quest Archetypes — TACo-Native

> **⚠️ Hypotheses** — These need testing with actual community managers. The question for each: does this solve a problem you actually have?

The key design principle: **quests should be things that are impossible or impractical without TACo.** If Galxe or Zealy could do it, it's not differentiated. TACo uniquely enables encryption-gated content, collective conditions, progressive on-chain trust, and cross-server reputation. The archetypes below lean into these.

Second principle: **amplify intrinsic motivation, don't replace it.** The [research is clear](https://home.ubalt.edu/tmitch/642/articles%20syllabus/Deci%20Koestner%20Ryan%20meta%20IM%20psy%20bull%2099.pdf) that monetary rewards for voluntary contributions can backfire. The best quests create *structures* that make intrinsically motivated people more effective, and use conditions to filter out mercenaries — rather than trying to bribe mercenaries into becoming contributors.

### 1. Collective Unlock — Encrypted Content That Requires Group Effort

A piece of valuable content — alpha, research, strategy, early access, dlc — is encrypted via TACo. It only decrypts when a group of participants has collectively met a set of conditions.

**How it works:**
- Community admin encrypts content (e.g., exclusive strategy guide, early feature access, alpha research, premium content)
- Decryption condition: N members must each independently complete a prerequisite (e.g., complete a tutorial, submit feedback, participate in a discussion)
- Nobody can access the content until enough people have genuinely contributed
- No single person can farm this — it requires real, distributed human participation

**Why it's new:** This is structurally impossible on Galxe/Zealy. Encryption-gated collective conditions don't exist anywhere else. It creates genuine cooperation rather than parallel farming.

**Satisfies:** knowledgeable contributors, onboarding that converts, sybil resistance
**TACo props:** collective oversight, auditable flows, guardrails
**Works for:** crypto (alpha/research access), gaming (early access to new content/mods), dev tooling (beta features), creative (exclusive sample packs or tutorials from well-known creators)

### 2. Progressive Trust Ladder — Earn Real Capabilities, Not Just Roles

A quest chain where completing stages grants escalating *on-chain capabilities*, not just Discord roles. Trust is earned through demonstrated behaviour and automatically revocable if violated.

**How it works:**
- **Stage 1:** Complete onboarding → can execute pre-approved transactions (e.g., claim rewards, vote on simple proposals)
- **Stage 2:** Sustained activity over N weeks → can propose transactions for community review
- **Stage 3:** Track record of good proposals → can approve others' transactions within limits
- **Stage 4:** Long-term trusted operator → elevated spending authority with collective oversight guardrails
- At every stage, conditions define what you can and can't do. Violation or inactivity automatically downgrades capabilities.

**Why it's new:** Discord roles are binary and trust-based. This is a continuous, on-chain, condition-enforced trust gradient with automatic revocation. No platform does this. It's what "trusted operators" looks like when you have programmable conditions.

**Satisfies:** trusted operators, onboarding that converts, knowledgeable contributors (the ladder incentivises learning)
**TACo props:** collective oversight, guardrails, cross-server portable identity, auditable flows
**Works for:** crypto DAOs (governance progression), gaming (tournament admin privileges), any community that needs to delegate safely

### 3. Blind Submission + Reveal — Encrypted Peer Review

Participants submit work encrypted. Submissions only decrypt after a deadline — preventing copying and enabling genuine blind review.

**How it works:**
- Quest: create something (strategy guide, mod, art, code contribution, governance proposal)
- Submit via TACo encryption with time-based decryption condition (unlocks at deadline)
- After deadline: all submissions decrypt simultaneously
- Community peer review on decrypted content (with tenure-gated voting to prevent sybil manipulation)
- Rewards based on peer assessment, not self-reporting

**Why it's new:** Time-locked encryption for submissions is something only TACo can provide in a decentralised way. It solves the copying problem that plagues every content creation quest, and creates genuine creative/intellectual competition.

**Satisfies:** knowledgeable contributors, quality feedback (the review process itself)
**TACo props:** auditable flows, mercenary avoidance (tenure-gated review), Discord-native
**Works for:** gaming (strategy contests, mod competitions), creative (remix challenges, jam sessions with blind submissions), dev tooling (hackathon-style sprints), crypto (governance proposal competitions)

### 4. Meritocratic Treasury — Earn Spending Authority

Community members earn increasing authority to spend from a shared treasury, with guardrails that automatically tighten or loosen based on track record. Not just multisig — *conditional delegation*.

**How it works:**
- Community treasury exists with collective oversight conditions
- New members: can request grants up to $X, requires M-of-N approval
- Established members: can spend up to $Y without approval for pre-approved categories (bounties, event prizes, etc.)
- Track record determines limits — successful grants that produce results → higher authority. Failed or disputed grants → reduced authority.
- All fully auditable, all condition-enforced, all automatically revocable.

**Why it's new:** Multisigs are all-or-nothing (you're a signer or you're not). This is graduated, conditional, and reputation-linked. It's what DAOs have always wanted but couldn't build — and it extends to non-crypto communities that manage any shared resources.

**Satisfies:** trusted operators, activity organisers (they get budgets for events), self-sustaining activity
**TACo props:** collective oversight (the entire point), guardrails, auditable flows, cross-server portable identity
**Works for:** crypto DAOs (grant committees, bounty funds), gaming (tournament prize pools), OSS (bounty management), any community with shared resources — even Patreon-funded ones

### 5. Reputation Staking — Vouch With Skin in the Game

Members can vouch for others by staking their own cross-server reputation. Creates accountability networks that make trust portable.

**How it works:**
- Established member vouches for a newcomer
- Voucher's reputation score is partially at risk
- If the newcomer performs well (completes quests, earns positive peer review), both benefit
- If the newcomer acts badly (sybil behaviour, bad-faith participation), voucher's score decreases
- Reputation is cross-server portable — vouching in one community affects your standing everywhere

**Why it's new:** No platform ties personal reputation to vouching in a verifiable, cross-server way. This creates genuine social accountability — the kind that already exists informally in tight-knit communities, but made portable and auditable.

**Satisfies:** sybil resistance, onboarding that converts (social onboarding via trusted members), trusted operators
**TACo props:** cross-server portable identity, auditable flows, conditions
**Works for:** crypto (anti-sybil for airdrops/governance), gaming (tournament integrity), any community where knowing who vouches for whom matters

---

## How Archetypes Map to Communities

| Archetype | Crypto/DAO | Dev Tooling | Gaming/Strategy | Creative |
|-----------|:----------:|:-----------:|:---------------:|:--------:|
| Collective Unlock | alpha/research | beta features | early content access | exclusive tutorials |
| Trust Ladder | governance progression | maintainer path | tournament admin | curator roles |
| Blind Submission | proposal contests | hackathons | strategy competitions | remix challenges |
| Meritocratic Treasury | grant committees | bounty funds | tournament prizes | shared project budgets |
| Reputation Staking | anti-sybil | contributor vouching | competitive integrity | — |

items.title: Video player controls not keyboard accessible
items.body:
## Accessibility Issue: Video player controls not keyboard accessible

**URL**: https://bottube.ai
**Issue type**: Keyboard navigation
**WCAG criterion**: 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A)

### Description
The video player controls (play/pause, volume, fullscreen, seek bar) cannot be operated using keyboard alone. Tab focus does not reach these controls, and there are no keyboard shortcuts documented.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Visit a video page on https://bottube.ai
2. Use Tab key to navigate
3. Attempt to control video playback with keyboard only
4. Observe: controls are not reachable or operable via keyboard

### Expected behavior
All video controls should be focusable and operable via keyboard (Tab, Enter, Space, arrow keys).

### Impact
Users who cannot use a mouse (motor disabilities) cannot control video playback.

**Claiming 1 RTC bounty from**: https://github.com/Scottcjn/rustchain-bounties/issues/1618
**RTC wallet**: RTC1c2d55419fbc69b7b1279130565b8994f3a04b9d

items.title: Security: 4 pending vulnerability reports - requesting preferred disclosure channel
items.body:
Hi LiteLLM team, I submitted four security reports via huntr on March 13th. None have been reviewed yet.

**Reports:**
- https://huntr.com/bounties/87b80d95-70e2-4df2-894f-c336ee2139fa
- https://huntr.com/bounties/2ae9dced-4927-4a54-b3ad-8da2d0bf9917
- https://huntr.com/bounties/5e944d61-017f-4f32-916d-2f3cae54f92e

Would you prefer I create GitHub Security Advisories for these instead, or is there a security contact I should reach out to directly?

Thanks,
[Regaan](https://github.com/regaan)

items.title: [BUG] [v1.0.0] compute_weights_from_issues drops issue counts due to inconsistent hotkey normalization
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

The function compute_weights_from_issues in src/scoring.rs can silently drop valid/invalid/duplicate/malicious issue counts for miners because it uses different hotkey representations when building its internal count maps versus when it looks those counts up. When iterating issues, it keys the valid_counts, invalid_counts, duplicate_counts, and malicious_counts maps using the raw hotkey string from IssueRecord.claimed_by_hotkey or storage::get_hotkey_by_github. Later, when iterating over storage::get_registered_hotkeys(), it calls ss58::normalize_hotkey and then looks up counts by the normalized string (hk) instead of the original string used as the map key. If the hotkeys stored in issue records or the GitHub index are not already in the normalized form (e.g., different SS58 representation or casing rules), all of that miner’s counts will resolve to zero in compute_weights_from_issues, resulting in incorrect net_points and therefore incorrect weight assignments for calculate_weights_from_leaderboard. This only affects the code path that recomputes weights from issues (intended for deterministic vTrust), making it subtle and hard to spot.

### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
OS: Ubuntu 22.04 (Linux 6.8.0-101-generic)
Repo version: bounty-challenge crate v0.1.0 (from Cargo.toml)
Source branch: main
```

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Steps to Reproduce

1. In a controlled test or local setup, ensure there is at least one registered hotkey H1 in on-chain storage via storage::register_user, so that storage::get_registered_hotkeys() returns ["H1"].
2. Ensure that issues synced into storage use a different string representation for the same hotkey (e.g., a non-normalized form) when populating IssueRecord.claimed_by_hotkey or via storage::get_hotkey_by_github, so that the keys stored in valid_counts/invalid_counts/etc. are not equal to ss58::normalize_hotkey("H1").
3. Call scoring::compute_weights_from_issues() (or exercise the code path that uses it to derive weights).
4. Observe that the returned WeightAssignment for the miner with hotkey H1 has weight == 0.0 (because net_points was computed from all-zero counts), despite the presence of valid/invalid/duplicate/malicious issues attributed to that miner in storage::get_synced_issues().
5. Compare this with what you would get if you temporarily remove the normalize_hotkey call and use the raw hotkey string as the map key in both the counting and lookup steps; the miner’s counts and resulting weight now reflect the actual issues.

### Expected Behavior

- compute_weights_from_issues should treat hotkeys consistently when:
      - Counting issues per hotkey into valid_counts, invalid_counts, duplicate_counts, and malicious_counts, and
      - Looking up those counts for each registered hotkey to build LeaderboardEntry values.
- If hotkey normalization is required, it should be applied once at the point where hotkeys are derived (both for issue - attribution and for the registered-hotkeys list), so that the same normalized string is used as the map key everywhere.
- A miner with valid/invalid/duplicate/malicious issues in the synced issue set should always see non-zero counts and weight that reflect those issues when compute_weights_from_issues is used.

### Actual Behavior

- compute_weights_from_issues builds the count maps keyed by the raw hotkey string taken from IssueRecord.claimed_by_hotkey or storage::get_hotkey_by_github(&issue.author).
- Later, when iterating storage::get_registered_hotkeys(), it calls ss58::normalize_hotkey(hotkey) and uses this normalized hk string both as the LeaderboardEntry.hotkey and as the key for valid_counts.get(&hk), invalid_counts.get(&hk), etc.
- If the raw hotkey strings used as map keys differ from the normalized representation, all lookups return None, so the miner’s valid, invalid, duplicate, and malicious counts are treated as zero, net_points is zero, and they effectively receive no weight from compute_weights_from_issues even though their issues exist and were correctly labeled.

### Additional Context

- This bug specifically affects the deterministic recomputation path that is supposed to compute weights purely from committed issue records (compute_weights_from_issues), which is important for validator consensus and vTrust.
- A minimal, non-breaking fix would be to normalize hotkeys consistently, e.g.:
      - Normalize the hotkey string before inserting into the count maps in the loop over all_issues, and
      - Normalize hotkeys uniformly when building hotkeys and when storing hotkeys in IssueRecord.claimed_by_hotkey and the GitHub index, so that the same canonical string is used throughout.
- Without this fix, validators using compute_weights_from_issues as a fallback or verification path can derive different effective weights compared to the normal balance-based path, and miners whose hotkeys are stored in a different (non-normalized) form can be under-rewarded.

items.title: [Epic]: Security, Risk & Audit (Phase 5)

milestone.title: Phase 5: Security & Audit
milestone.description: Fuzz testing, invariant tests, formal verification, external audit, pen-testing

items.body:
## Objective

Harden the protocol against financial exploits, session key compromise, oracle manipulation, and latency arbitrage — then get an external audit to build confidence for real money deployment.

## Rationale

This is a leveraged financial protocol where bugs mean lost money. Session keys add attack surface. The oracle is a single point of failure. Without rigorous security, mainnet deployment is irresponsible.

## Scope

**In scope:**
- Fuzz testing: 50k+ runs per function (Foundry)
- Invariant testing: solvency guarantee under all conditions
- Formal verification: Certora/Halmos for critical invariants
- Session key pen-testing: escalation, replay, exceed bounds
- Oracle sanity: TWAP checks, spike rejection, staleness detection
- Liquidation bot (Rust): monitors positions, liquidates when margin < maintenance
- Risk management: position limits, max OI per side, circuit breaker, house reserve monitoring
- Frontend CSP (Content-Security-Policy)
- Dependency audits: `pnpm audit` + `cargo audit`
- External audit engagement

**Out of scope:**
- Mainnet deployment (Phase 6 — separate epic)
- Bug bounty program setup
- Formal KYC/AML compliance

## Architecture & technical considerations

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Risk Layer
        PL[Position Limits] --> PE[PerpEngine]
        CB[Circuit Breaker] --> PE
        HR[House Reserve Monitor] --> PE
        OC[Oracle Sanity\nTWAP + Staleness] --> PO[PriceOracle]
    end
    
    subgraph Keeper Infrastructure
        LB[Liquidation Bot\nRust] -->|monitors| PE
        LB -->|liquidate| PE
    end
    
    subgraph Security Testing
        FZ[Fuzz Testing\n50k runs] --> PE
        IV[Invariant Tests\nSolvency] --> PE
        FV[Formal Verification\nCertora] --> PE
        PT[Pen Testing\nSession Keys] --> SKV[SessionKeyValidator]
    end
```

Critical invariant: `total_payouts <= total_collateral + house_reserve` — must hold under ALL conditions.

## Dependencies

- Depends on: All previous epics (need a working system to audit)
- Blocks: Mainnet deployment

## Issue breakdown

- [ ] Fuzz testing suite: 50k runs per public function
- [ ] Invariant test: solvency guarantee
- [ ] Formal verification with Certora or Halmos
- [ ] Session key pen-testing: escalation, replay, bound exceed
- [ ] Oracle sanity: TWAP check, spike rejection (> 3 sigma)
- [ ] Liquidation bot (Rust): position monitoring + auto-liquidation
- [ ] Position size limits per user
- [ ] Max open interest per side (long/short) with circuit breaker
- [ ] House reserve monitoring with auto-pause
- [ ] Frontend Content-Security-Policy
- [ ] Dependency audit: pnpm audit + cargo audit
- [ ] External audit: engagement, scope definition, remediation

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Zero critical/high findings unresolved after remediation
- [ ] Solvency invariant formally verified
- [ ] Session key cannot exceed bounds under any tested scenario
- [ ] Oracle rejects price spikes > 3 sigma from TWAP
- [ ] Liquidation bot liquidates underwater positions within 1 block (10ms)
- [ ] Circuit breaker pauses new positions when OI imbalance exceeds threshold
- [ ] House reserve auto-pause triggers when reserve < minimum
- [ ] CSP blocks inline scripts and unauthorized domains
- [ ] Zero known vulnerabilities in dependencies
- [ ] External audit report received with all critical/high findings addressed

items.title: ⚡ Bounty: RTC/ERG Trading Pair on Spectrum DEX (150 RTC)
items.body:
## RTC/ERG Trading Pair — Spectrum DEX Integration

Enable real price discovery for RTC by creating a trading pair with ERG on Spectrum Finance (Ergo DEX).

### Background

RustChain already has Ergo integration:
- Private Ergo chain running at 50.28.86.131
- Miner attestations anchored to Ergo blocks
- ERG has real USD value (~$1.50)

### Requirements

**1. Token Issuance on Ergo**
- Issue RTC as an Ergo native token
- 1:1 backing with RustChain RTC
- Mint contract that verifies RustChain balance

**2. Bridge Contract**
- Lock RTC on RustChain → Mint eRTC on Ergo
- Burn eRTC on Ergo → Unlock RTC on RustChain
- Simple 2-of-3 multisig or threshold signature

**3. Spectrum DEX Pool**
- Create RTC/ERG liquidity pool on https://app.spectrum.fi
- Initial liquidity: ~1,000 RTC + equivalent ERG
- Set initial price: 1 RTC = 0.067 ERG (~$0.10 at $1.50/ERG)

**4. Documentation**
- How to bridge RTC ↔ eRTC
- How to trade on Spectrum
- Liquidity provider guide

### Technical Notes

Ergo token issuance uses EIP-4 standard:
```json
{
  "name": "RustChain Token",
  "symbol": "RTC",
  "decimals": 6,
  "description": "Bridged RTC from RustChain PoA network"
}
```

Spectrum pool creation requires:
- Token ID for RTC
- Initial ERG deposit
- Pool creation transaction

### Bounty: 150 RTC (~$15)

**Milestones:**
1. Token issuance contract (50 RTC)
2. Bridge contract + UI (50 RTC)
3. Spectrum pool live + docs (50 RTC)

### Resources
- Spectrum Finance: https://spectrum.fi
- Ergo Token Minting: https://docs.ergoplatform.com/dev/tokens/
- RustChain Ergo Anchor: See `/root/rustchain/ergo_miner_anchor.py`

---
*Real liquidity for real hardware mining* 🦀💱

items.title: [Bounty] @tomisnotcat — ARM architecture details needed (5 RTC)
items.body:
Hey @tomisnotcat — welcome to RustChain!

We noticed your miner is attesting with `device_family: arm` but `device_arch: modern`. Our fingerprint system needs more detail about your hardware to assign the correct antiquity multiplier.

**What we need (5 RTC bounty):**

Please reply with the output of these commands on the machine running your miner:

```bash
# 1. CPU info
cat /proc/cpuinfo | head -30

# 2. Architecture
uname -a

# 3. Device model (if applicable)
cat /sys/firmware/devicetree/base/model 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A"

# 4. CPU details
lscpu | grep -i "model\|arch\|vendor\|cpu"
```

**Why this matters:**
ARM covers everything from a $5 Raspberry Pi Zero to a $3,000 Ampere server. We need to know which so we can:
1. Set the right reward multiplier for your hardware class
2. Add your specific ARM variant to our fingerprint detection

If you're running something unique (vintage ARM, dev board, etc.) there may be an antiquity bonus available.

**Current status:** Your miner is attesting and enrolled, but at minimum ARM weight (0.001x) until we can verify the hardware class.

Thanks for mining!
